item.title: How AI is forcing McKinsey and its peers to rethink pricing
item.description: Clients are questioning the value of advice while getting more used to fees based on successful task completion

item.title: Iran’s Guards used UAE company to buy military satellite equipment
item.description: Records seen by FT show Tehran used procurement network in Gulf state that it later hit with missiles and drones

item.title: Commerzbank rallies shareholders in fight for independence from UniCredit
item.description: At its annual meeting this week, the German lender’s long-suffering shareholders finally had something to cheer

item.title: Biggest UK mobile groups suffer worst year of customer losses
item.description: Incumbents are increasingly ceding ground to virtual rivals who pay to access their network

item.title: Gap between London house prices and other UK cities at narrowest since 2009
item.description: Affordability pressures weigh on capital while Manchester, Liverpool and other regional hubs benefit from population growth

item.title: French nicotine pouch ban is ‘attack on Swedish way of life’, minister says
item.description: Stockholm smoulders over France’s ‘absurd’ penalties of up to five years in prison for cigarette alternatives

item.title: ‘Kidults’ fuel Japan’s ‘gacha gacha’ capsule toy mania
item.description: About 1,400 stores have popped up in shops left empty by Covid-era closures

item.title: Turkish Airlines targets top spot after winning traffic from Gulf rivals
item.description: Istanbul hub remains fully operational during conflict that has disrupted carriers in the region, chair Murat Seker says

item.title: The power struggle in the world’s narrow seas
item.description: As the Strait of Hormuz crisis grips the global economy, nations are vying to secure other vulnerable waterways

item.title: US warns Japan of severe delays in Tomahawk deliveries due to Iran war
item.description: Missiles could come two years late as Pentagon works to replenish stocks depleted in Middle East military campaign

item.title: Starmer urges TNT to screen Champions League final for free
item.description: UK prime minister says football fans should not have to pay to watch Arsenal-PSG match next weekend

item.title: Uber and DoorDash sound out investors for Delivery Hero bid
item.description: American food delivery rivals seek to acquire stake in the German group as they consider a takeover

item.title: US oil producers increase output to capture price surge from Iran war
item.description: Drilling expansion follows 40 per cent jump in cost from global supply crunch that has dented president’s approval ratings

item.title: SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs set to test limits of AI boom
item.description: Elon Musk, Sam Altman and Dario Amodei battle over who can command Wall Street’s deepest pools of capital

item.title: BYD’s long and winding road towards the F1 grid
item.description: Also in today’s newsletter: the ‘spygate’ scandal rocking English football

item.title: Swatch and Audemars Piguet’s collab provides a much-needed pop
item.description: The launch may not have gone quite according to plan, but such tie-ups are a good way to inject some buzz into the market

item.title: US crime and mortality is declining fast — what can the rest of us learn?
item.description: America is making progress in driving down violent deaths and those from some dangerous diseases

item.title: How AI has changed M&A
item.description: The size of deals is hitting new peaks, unloved companies are becoming sexy and PE has found a new gold mine

item.title: Get ready to take sides in Big Tech’s giant IPO bake-off
item.description: With SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic targeting the same market, investors must decide what qualities they prize most

item.title: Superdrug owner shrugs off volatility to press ahead with $30bn dual listing
item.description: AS Watson plans to publicly trade shares in both Hong Kong and London before the end of 2026

item.title: The new Luddite movement
item.description: If governments don’t slow AI down, voters — like their predecessors — might take matters into their own hands

item.title: The new arms race in computing power
item.description: Military capability depends increasingly on data centres. Now governments outpaced in AI are looking to experimental technologies

item.title: Britain curbs electricity trading after straining Europe’s power grids
item.description: Change in practice means state-owned operator NESO may need to rely more heavily on gas-fired power stations

item.title: Time to ‘do the splits’ on your mortgage?
item.description: Overstretched borrowers are finding ways to alleviate the pain of higher interest rates

item.title: China Inc goes shopping for western consumer brands
item.description: Companies seek growth abroad through deals for Everlane and Puma amid domestic competition and deflationary pressures

item.title: Air travel sucks — now more than ever
item.description: When it comes to getting on a plane, you cannot lower your expectations enough

item.title: Classical music has survived for centuries. Will AI kill it?
item.description: Composers have always experimented with new technology — but the latest advances threaten ‘skill death’ in this centuries-old art form

item.title: Britain’s EV boom hits second-hand bump in the road
item.description: Automakers and banks are grappling with how to manage the residual value of electric cars

item.title: The dos and don’ts of travel insurance when you’re over 50
item.description: Avoid any issues by declaring all pre-existing medical conditions

item.title: SpaceX launches redesigned Starship in successful pre-IPO test
item.description: Elon Musk’s company demonstrates the technology at the heart of the biggest initial public offering in history
